title: Antony Villa
content:
آ آ آ آ Over there, above the jetty, stands the mansion of the Vardens,
آ آ آ آ With a tennis ground and terrace, and a flagstaff in the gardens:
آ آ آ آ They are gentlemen and ladies, theyï؟½ve been ï؟½toffsï؟½ for generations,
آ آ آ آ But old Vardenï؟½s been unlucky, lost a lot in speculations.

آ آ آ آ Troubles gathered fast upon him when the mining bubble ï؟½busted,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then the bank suspended payment, where his little all he trusted;
آ آ آ آ And the butcher and the baker sent their bills in when they read it,
آ آ آ آ Even John, the Chow that served him, has refused to give him ï؟½cledit.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And the daughters of the Vardens, they are beautiful as Graces, 
آ آ آ آ But the balconyï؟½s deserted, and they rarely show their faces;
آ آ آ آ And the swells of their acquaintance never seem to venture near them,
آ آ آ آ And the bailiff says they seldom have a cup of tea to cheer them.

آ آ آ آ They were butterflies, I always was a common caterpillar,
آ آ آ آ But Iï؟½m sorry for the ladies over there in ï؟½Tony Villa,
آ آ آ آ Shut up there in ï؟½Tony Villa with the bailiff and their trouble;
آ آ آ آ And the dried-up reservoir, where my tears were seems to bubble.

آ آ آ آ Mrs. Rooney thinks it nothing when she sends a brat to ï؟½borryï؟½
آ آ آ آ Just a pinch of tea and sugar till the grocer comes ï؟½temorry;ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But itï؟½s difï؟½rent with the Vardens, they would starve to death as soon as
آ آ آ آ Knuckle down. You know, they werenï؟½t raised exactly like the Rooneys!

آ آ آ آ There is gossip in the ï؟½boxesï؟½ and the drawing-rooms and gardens, 
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Have you heard of Vardenï؟½s failure? Have you heard about the Vardens?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And no doubt each toney mother on the Point across the waterï؟½s
آ آ آ آ Mighty glad about the downfall of the rivals of her daughters.

آ آ آ آ (Thoï؟½ the poets and the writers say that man to manï؟½s inhuman,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m inclined to think itï؟½s nothing to what woman is to woman,
آ آ آ آ More especially, the ladies, save perhaps a fellowï؟½s mother;
آ آ آ آ And I think that men are better, they are kinder to each other.)

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a youngster by the jetty gathering cinders from the ashes,
آ آ آ آ He was known as ï؟½Master Vardenï؟½ ere the great financial crashes.
آ آ آ آ And his manner shows the difï؟½rence ï؟½twixt the nursï؟½ry and gutter, 
آ آ آ آ But Iï؟½ve seen him at the grocerï؟½s buying half a pound of butter.

آ آ آ آ And his mother fights her trouble in the house across the water,
آ آ آ آ She is just as proud as Varden, though she was a ï؟½cockyï؟½sï؟½ daughter;
آ آ آ آ And at times I think I see her with the flickï؟½ring firelight oï؟½er her,
آ آ آ آ Sitting pale and straight and quiet, gazing vacantly before her.

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a slight and girlish figure, Vardenï؟½s youngest daughter, Nettie, 
آ آ آ آ On the terrace after sunset, when the boat is near the jetty;
آ آ آ آ She is good and pure and pretty, and her rivals donï؟½t deny it,
آ آ آ آ Though they say that Nettie Varden takes in sewing on the quiet.

آ آ آ آ (How her sister graced the ï؟½circle,ï؟½ all unconscious of a lover
آ آ آ آ In the seedy ï؟½godï؟½ who watched her from the gallery above her!
آ آ آ آ Shade of Poverty was on him, and the light of Wealth upon her,
آ آ آ آ But perhaps he loved her better than the swells attending on her.)

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a white manï؟½s heart in Varden, spite of all the blue blood in him,
آ آ آ آ There are working men who wouldnï؟½t stand and hear a word aginï؟½ him;
آ آ آ آ But his name was never printed by the side of his ï؟½donations,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Save on hearts that have, in this world, very humble circulations.

آ آ آ آ He was never stiff or hoggish, he was affable and jolly,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½d always say ï؟½Good morningï؟½ to the deck hand on the ï؟½Polly;ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He would ï؟½barrackï؟½ with the newsboys on the Quay across the ferry,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½d very often tip ï؟½em coming home a trifle merry.

آ آ آ آ But his chin is getting higher, and his features daily harden
آ آ آ آ (He will not ï؟½give up possessionï؟½, thereï؟½s a lot of fight in Varden);
آ آ آ آ And the way he steps the gangway! oh, you couldnï؟½t but admire it!
آ آ آ آ Just as proud as ever hero walked the plank aboard a pirate!

آ آ آ آ He will think about the hardships that his girls were never ï؟½useter,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And it must be mighty heavy on the thoroughbred old rooster;
آ آ آ آ But heï؟½ll never strike his colours, and I tell a lying tale if
آ آ آ آ Vardenï؟½s pride donï؟½t kill him sooner than the bankers or the bailiff.

آ آ آ آ You remember when we often had to go without our dinners,
آ آ آ آ In the days when Pride and Hunger fought a finish out within us;
آ آ آ آ And how Pride would come up groggy, Hunger whooping loud and louder, 
آ آ آ آ And the swells are proud as we are; they are just as proud, and prouder.

آ آ آ آ Yes, the toffs have grit, in spite of all our sneering and our scorning, 
آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s the crowd? Whatï؟½s that? God help us!, Varden shot himself this morning! . . . .
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½ll be gossip in the ï؟½circle,ï؟½ in the drawing-rooms and gardens;
آ آ آ آ But Iï؟½m sorry for the family; yes, Iï؟½m sorry for the Vardens.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
